summary:
The Luba Kingdom (c. 1585–1889 AD) was one of the most sophisticated states in Central African history — a Bantu-speaking polity centered in the Katanga region of modern Democratic Republic of Congo that developed distinctive political institutions, artistic traditions, and a system of royal memory known as lukasa that encoded historical and genealogical knowledge in physical objects. Founded according to tradition by the hero Mbidi Kiluwe (whose civilizing mission from the east introduced divine kingship to the Luba people), the kingdom was organized around the concept of bulopwe — a sacred royal power transmitted through royal bloodlines but requiring periodic renewal through ritual. The king was considered a divine intermediary, and elaborate ceremonies surrounded his installation, daily life, and death.

Luba political culture was remarkable for its portability: when princes were sent to govern distant territories or to establish client kingdoms, they carried the political forms and ideological frameworks of Luba kingship with them, spreading Luba institutions across a wide area — the 'Luba-Lunda' cultural sphere that influenced states across Central Africa. The lukasa memory boards, decorated with beads and shells, were read by initiated memory specialists who could recite complex historical narratives encoded in the objects' spatial arrangements — a sophisticated non-alphabetic information storage system. The Luba kingdom reached its greatest extent in the 18th century, incorporating long-distance trade networks in copper and salt, before being destabilized by the [[event:2504|East African slave trade]] and ultimately conquered by Belgian colonial forces in the late 19th century.
